payload.query: Which came first, the electoral system or the party system? Explain the relationship between electoral rules and party systems.

articles.title: Bangladesh Has Exposed Democracy’s Deeper Problem
articles.abstract: Toppling autocrats is easier than rebuilding the institutions democracy depends on.
articles.snippet: Toppling autocrats is easier than rebuilding the institutions democracy depends on.
articles.byline: By Zahid Hussain and Tom Felix Joehnk

articles.title: Syria Chooses a Parliament of Revolutionaries
articles.abstract: In the first elections since the Assad regime was ousted, there was no popular vote and women and minorities won few seats. Still, many Syrians saw it as progress toward ending authoritarian rule.
articles.snippet: In the first elections since the Assad regime was ousted, there was no popular vote and women and minorities won few seats. Still, many Syrians saw it as progress toward ending authoritarian rule.

articles.title: Malawi’s Former President Set to Return to Power, Early Results Show
articles.abstract: Peter Mutharika, an 85-year-old Yale-educated law professor, looked on course to defeat the incumbent, Lazarus Chakwera.
articles.snippet: Peter Mutharika, an 85-year-old Yale-educated law professor, looked on course to defeat the incumbent, Lazarus Chakwera.
articles.byline: By Golden Matonga and John Eligon

articles.title: Trump Empowers Election Deniers, Still Fixated on 2020 Grievances
articles.abstract: The president has placed proponents of his false claims into government jobs while dismantling systems built to secure voting, raising fears that he aims to seize authority over elections ahead of next year’s midterms.
articles.snippet: The president has placed proponents of his false claims into government jobs while dismantling systems built to secure voting, raising fears that he aims to seize authority over elections ahead of next year’s midterms.
articles.byline: By Alexandra Berzon and Nick Corasaniti

articles.title: Redistricting Battles, Set Off by Trump, Have Few Parallels in U.S. History
articles.abstract: First, Texas redistricted. Other states followed, and now California. Some legal experts say it’s a crisis: “The wheels are coming off the car right now.”
articles.snippet: First, Texas redistricted. Other states followed, and now California. Some legal experts say it’s a crisis: “The wheels are coming off the car right now.”
articles.byline: By Richard Fausset and Nick Corasaniti

articles.title: They Used to Rule the West. Now They’re Dying.
articles.abstract: The decline of traditional political parties is the precondition for our hyperpolitical age.
articles.snippet: The decline of traditional political parties is the precondition for our hyperpolitical age.

articles.title: Should New York City Ditch Its Party Primaries in Favor of Open Races?
articles.abstract: A panel created by Mayor Eric Adams wants to consider using an “open primary” system for elections in New York City. Here’s how the plan would work.
articles.snippet: A panel created by Mayor Eric Adams wants to consider using an “open primary” system for elections in New York City. Here’s how the plan would work.

articles.title: Trumpism Will Be With Us for a Very Long Time
articles.abstract: Why the future of the G.O.P. will likely be guided by the right’s political DNA.
articles.snippet: Why the future of the G.O.P. will likely be guided by the right’s political DNA.

articles.title: From 2013: Margaret Thatcher, ‘Iron Lady’ Who Set Britain on New Course, Dies at 87
articles.abstract: She held the office of prime minister longer than any other British politician in the 20th century, setting her country on a right-leaning economic path.
articles.snippet: She held the office of prime minister longer than any other British politician in the 20th century, setting her country on a right-leaning economic path.

articles.title: States Say No Thanks to Trump Tax Cuts, Drawing Republican Fire
articles.abstract: State income taxes often reflect federal policies, but Washington, D.C. and some states have taken steps to keep the latest Republican changes out of their tax codes.
articles.snippet: State income taxes often reflect federal policies, but Washington, D.C. and some states have taken steps to keep the latest Republican changes out of their tax codes.
